description:
You are acting as a database-reliability engineer who has been asked to tidy up
  an ad-hoc backup area and generate a short compression report.

  Initial set-up (already on disk):
    1. Directory “/home/user/db_backups” **already exists** and contains exactly
       three uncompressed SQL dumps:
         • /home/user/db_backups/customers.sql
         • /home/user/db_backups/orders.sql
         • /home/user/db_backups/inventory.sql

    2. There is **no** directory called “/home/user/backup_logs” yet.

  Goal (what you must achieve from the terminal):

    A. Compress every “*.sql” file that currently lives directly under
       “/home/user/db_backups/” using gzip, deleting the original “*.sql” files
       in the process (the default behaviour of gzip when no “-k” switch is
       supplied).

       • After you are finished, “/home/user/db_backups/” must contain three
         files named:
            customers.sql.gz
            orders.sql.gz
            inventory.sql.gz
         and **no** “.sql” files.

    B. Create the directory “/home/user/backup_logs”.

    C. Generate one single plain-text log file called
       “/home/user/backup_logs/compression_report.log”.
       The file MUST follow *exactly* the format below so that automated tests
       can validate it byte-for-byte:

          1. One record line per backup file in **lexicographic order of the
             original file names** (customers, inventory, orders).  Each record
             line must be formatted like:

                 filename: ORIGINAL=<bytes> COMPRESSED=<bytes>

             • “filename” is the basename that originally ended with “.sql”
               (for example, customers.sql).
             • “ORIGINAL” is the size in **bytes** of that .sql file *before*
               it was gzipped.  Obtain it with a tool such as “stat -c%s”.
             • “COMPRESSED” is the size in **bytes** of the corresponding
               “.sql.gz” file *after* compression.

          2. After the three record lines, write a single summary line using
             the exact wording and spacing shown:

                 TOTAL ORIGINAL=<bytes> TOTAL COMPRESSED=<bytes> SAVINGS=<bytes>

             • “TOTAL ORIGINAL” is the sum of all ORIGINAL byte counts.
             • “TOTAL COMPRESSED” is the sum of all COMPRESSED byte counts.
             • “SAVINGS” is TOTAL ORIGINAL minus TOTAL COMPRESSED.

       An *exact* concrete example of the target structure is shown below
       (the numbers will of course differ on your run, but the *layout* must be
       identical):

           customers.sql: ORIGINAL=999 COMPRESSED=321
           inventory.sql: ORIGINAL=888 COMPRESSED=222
           orders.sql: ORIGINAL=777 COMPRESSED=111
           TOTAL ORIGINAL=2664 TOTAL COMPRESSED=654 SAVINGS=2010

       Do **NOT** introduce blank lines, extra spaces, tabs or any additional
       headers/footers.  Only these four lines should be present.

    D. Ensure that every numeric field really is a pure base-10 integer (no
       thousand separators, no “bytes” suffix, etc.).

  Strict verification criteria the automated grader will apply:

    1. /home/user/db_backups/ must contain three “.gz” files and **zero** “.sql”
       files.
    2. /home/user/backup_logs/compression_report.log must exist and be readable.
    3. The log file must have exactly four newline-terminated lines and match
       the formatting rules above, including lexicographic ordering.
    4. The ORIGINAL size fields must match the true byte size of each of the
       original .sql files that existed at the start of the task.
    5. For every record line, the COMPRESSED size must be **strictly smaller**
       than the corresponding ORIGINAL size.
    6. The totals in the summary line must be arithmetically correct.

  Important constraints / tips:

    • You must rely only on standard GNU utilities already present in a typical
      Linux environment (find, xargs, stat, gzip, mkdir, echo, printf, sort,
      etc.).
    • The whole operation can be achieved with just a couple of concise
      find/xargs pipelines plus a small shell snippet to build the log file.
    • You do **not** have root privileges; stay within /home/user.
    • Be sure you create the “backup_logs” directory before trying to place the
      report inside it, otherwise the tests will fail.
    • Once you are done, read the compression_report.log back to the console
      so a human reviewer can eyeball it easily (this is optional but often
      useful).

  Your mission is complete when all six verification criteria above pass.
truth:
Pre-existing files and their byte-accurate contents before the agent starts:

    /home/user/db_backups/customers.sql
        -- Customers table backup
        INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1,'Alice');

        Byte size (including the trailing newline): 68

    /home/user/db_backups/orders.sql
        -- Orders table backup
        INSERT INTO orders VALUES (101,1,'2023-01-01');

        Byte size: 71

    /home/user/db_backups/inventory.sql
        -- Inventory table backup
        INSERT INTO inventory VALUES (1001,'Widget',50);

        Byte size: 75

  The automated checker will remember those ORIGINAL sizes:
      customers.sql → 68
      orders.sql    → 71
      inventory.sql → 75
      TOTAL ORIGINAL = 214

  After successful completion the checker will assert:

    • The set of files inside /home/user/db_backups is exactly:
        customers.sql.gz
        orders.sql.gz
        inventory.sql.gz

    • /home/user/backup_logs/compression_report.log exists and matches the
      following regular-expression pattern (POSIX extended), where each “[0-9]+”
      token stands for a non-empty sequence of digits:

        ^customers\.sql: ORIGINAL=68 COMPRESSED=([0-9]+)\n
        inventory\.sql: ORIGINAL=75 COMPRESSED=([0-9]+)\n
        orders\.sql: ORIGINAL=71 COMPRESSED=([0-9]+)\n
        TOTAL ORIGINAL=214 TOTAL COMPRESSED=([0-9]+) SAVINGS=([0-9]+)\n$

      and, additionally, it will evaluate that every captured “COMPRESSED”
      number is < its respective ORIGINAL and that the “TOTAL” arithmetic is
      correct.

  No other directories or files should be created.
